Ergonomics Field Research Co-Design Published

Before the Tool,
There Was the Hand

An ergonomic intervention for tyre cutters in Jabalpur, India — designed to reduce injury without erasing what they already know.

Tyre cutters in Jabalpur have a 6-month apprenticeship before they touch the blade. Their tools are forged by a blacksmith 45 minutes away. Their skill is held in the wrist, trained over years. This project asked how an ergonomic intervention could reduce injury without erasing what they already know.
ContextTyre cutting units, Jabalpur, Madhya Pradesh OutputErgonomic hand tool — published SAGE journals (2024)
30% reduction in RULA / RSI strain scores
Read the full story
01 — Introduction & Overview

Design Intervention for Reduction of Risk of Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders in Tyre Cutting Units in Jabalpur, India

Design Intervention for Reduction of Risk of Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders in Tyre Cutting Units in Jabalpur, India
Reduced 30% Strain Index Score among the Tyre Cutters in Jabalpur

Methodology

  • Observations
  • Interviews
  • Photography
  • Videography
  • Postural Analysis

Design Process (6 Months)

  • Brainstorming
  • Use of Analogy
  • Prototyping
  • Workstation Design
  • Hand Tool Design
  • Testing

Results / Findings

RULA scores consistently high at 7. RSI scores for right hand significantly above 10. Workers reported incidents of slips with the primitive tool design leading to severe cuts.

Work-related musculoskeletal disorders (WMSDs) are a significant issue that affects individuals' ability to work and can lead to time away from work for recovery. This not only burdens the individuals monetarily and impacts their caregivers and families but also affects employers and the economy.

While factors like age, BMI, working hours, and work experience can be associated with WMSDs, studies in informal sectors using manual labor often employ tools like the Rapid Upper Limb Assessment (RULA) and Rapid Entire Body Assessment (REBA) to pinpoint factors contributing to WMSD risk.

In India, small-scale industries (SSIs) frequently use hand tools, especially in the unorganized sector. Workers are particularly susceptible to WMSDs, with approximately 24% of reported hand-tool injuries being WMSDs.

02 — Jabalpur Tyre Cutters

Workers, Tools & Context

The tyre cutting units in Jabalpur represent an unorganized sector where old tyres are manually cut into smaller pieces using locally available hand tools. This work involves forceful exertions and skillful movements like piercing, cutting, and separating rubber tyre parts.

Workers, including youth who initially observe for about six months due to the danger of the work, perform this with tools forged by blacksmiths located far away — making sharpening infrequent and work less efficient.

Studies using RSI and RULA confirm that the tasks performed are in a high-risk category, necessitating immediate ergonomic intervention.

03 — Project Aim

Redesigning the Existing Hand Tool

The primary aim of this project was to redesign the existing hand tool used by tyre cutters to reduce the physical strain on the workers and, consequently, lower the risk of developing WMSDs. The images above show the original blade (a) and the uncomfortable grip it forced on workers (b).

04 — Methodology

Research Approach

Observation

The method of Drury (1995) was used for direct observation and activity analysis of the various tasks involved in the tyre cutting process — understanding each step, recording tools used, their dimensions, the workers' grip, postures, workspace, movements, repetitions, exhaustion levels, temperature, lighting, and sunlight exposure.

Interview

Interviews were conducted with the workers (following Miller & Crabtree, 1992) to understand their perspectives on their work, perceived exhaustion and pain levels, hazards and safety incidents, training time, economic conditions, and other influencing factors.

Photography & Videography

Photography and videography were used in conjunction with direct observation to objectively evaluate worker postures, wrist angles, body elevations, tyre and tool sizes, and workspace areas.

Postural Analysis

The non-invasive postural analysis tools RULA (McAtamney & Corlett, 1993) and RSI (Revised Strain Index; Garg et al., 2016) were employed to further establish the urgent need for intervention by analyzing the observed postures.

05 — Design Process

Empathise → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test

06 — Findings Before Intervention

Current Situation — Activity Analysis

Activity analysis revealed several causes for poor posture while sharpening the tool, cutting small tyres, and cutting big tyres.

07 — Workstation Design (Attempted)

Inclined Workstation Prototype

An inclined workstation was designed considering the anthropometric dimensions of the workers, with a 53° incline to encourage better posture by reducing forward bending. However, this design was not well-received by the workers due to the weight of the tyres making lifting impractical, their preference for rolling tyres, and significant friction hindering rotation on the inclined surface.

08 — Hand Tool Design — Ideation

Final Ideations

Ideation explored a perpendicular handle for better control, shortening the blade to bring the force application point closer to the hand, and a rotating tyre table. Analogies from other indigenous hand tools were drawn to inspire grip and blade geometry.

09 — Before & After

The Redesigned Hand Tool

Before
Original blade — full length, forces the palm into awkward, forceful, repetitive grip postures
After
Blade shortened to half · rotatable 90° · T-shaped handle · manufactured by local blacksmiths & woodworkers
Blade

Shortened to half its original length to bring the force application point closer to the hand.

Rotation

Made rotatable by 90° to accommodate different cutting grips and reduce wrist deviation.

Handle

T-shaped for power grip. Dimensions based on worker anthropometrics. Rubberized for comfort & temperature insulation.

10 — Testing & Results

Impact of the New Hand Tool

30%
Reduction in RSI Strain — Right Hand

Testing showed that the new hand tool alone led to a significant decrease in RSI scores. The redesigned handheld tool was well-received by the workers.

Left hand
↓ 14.78% (14.2→12.1)
Right hand
↓ 30.58% (41.2→28.6)
11 — Conclusion
The tyre cutters were very open to improvements and interventions to help improve their work processes.

The hand tool alone, if adopted, could bring significant relief in their work practice. This reiterates the need to foray into the informal sector and the working conditions in a gradual manner — as even starting off with smaller tools can lead to big help in decreasing the risk of WMSDs for the workers in this sector.

This project demonstrates that ergonomic design need not erase existing skill and tacit knowledge. By working with the workers — not for them — the intervention could be adopted with minimal disruption to their existing practice.

Published
SAGE Journals, 2024
Assessment
RULA / RSI Reduction
Location
Jabalpur, MP, India
